comments.author: RebarBotNava
comments.body: Plan-review PASS (signed, --ref HEAD so code-grounding sees arcticduck's committed helper) on iteration 5. Key correction driven by the review: empirically verified Agent(tool_timeout=) CANCELS async tools (~0.3s) but is a NO-OP for sync in-process tools (async cancel can't stop a blocking call) — rebar's tools are sync, so tool_timeout bounds MCP/async tools; sync ones bounded by derived step caps. Also deduplicated: read timeout reuses cfg.timeout_s (no new key), authoritative on anthropic path (our client) vs model_settings['timeout'] for other providers. Implemented + committed 2b72bf60a. LLMConfig.llm_tool_timeout_s(120); httpx.Timeout via arcticduck http_timeout seam; ADR 0038 (idle-watchdog deferred). 8 timeout tests + 178 affected green; lint+typecheck clean. Not closing until Verified +1. NOTE: children after arcticduck must be reviewed with --ref HEAD (default origin/main doesn't include committed stack code).

compiled_state.description:
Activity-based liveness for LLM gate ops using `agent.run_sync()`-compatible mechanisms: a per-request READ timeout (bounds a hung MODEL, at the HTTP layer) + a per-TOOL timeout (bounds a hung ASYNC/MCP tool), layered on the step caps the runner ALREADY derives. NO total-runtime timeout, NO async stream-event watchdog (deferred).

## Why
A single hard total-runtime timeout truncates healthy multi-minute / large-context (1M-token) runs; liveness must be gauged by ACTIVITY, not total elapsed time. But the runner uses `agent.run_sync()` exclusively — a blocking synchronous call with no concurrent stream-event hooks. The async idle-watchdog pattern (LangGraph/Claude-SDK) needs `agent.run()`/`run_stream_events` + an event loop this runner does not have. So liveness is achieved at request+tool granularity WITHOUT asyncio.

## What
- **Per-request READ timeout (both execution modes — `single_turn` structured calls AND `agentic` tool-using runs) — the PRIMARY, verified mechanism.** Set `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)` (a total of `cfg.timeout_s` with a tight connect) on the shared `httpx.AsyncClient` story arcticduck constructs, passed in via the EXISTING seam `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(..., http_timeout=<httpx.Timeout>)` (arcticduck committed that param in `runner.py` — review this story with `--ref` at the stack HEAD). This REUSES `cfg.timeout_s` (already the per-request budget, today wired only to `model_settings['timeout']`) — NO new `llm_request_timeout_s` key (would duplicate it). Bounds a HUNG MODEL (no bytes) — the real, common failure. VERIFIED under `run_sync` by arcticduck's spike (an httpx read/timeout fires at the transport). It is Anthropic-path-only (OpenAI/Google build their own httpx clients — acceptable; the gates run on Anthropic). NO duplication with `model_settings['timeout']`: on the ANTHROPIC path rebar uses arcticduck's CUSTOM `httpx.AsyncClient`, so THIS client's `httpx.Timeout` is the authoritative per-request bound; the pre-existing `model_settings['timeout'] = float(cfg.timeout_s)` (runner.py:288-289) governs the OTHER providers (OpenAI/Google), which use pydantic-ai's internally-built client, not ours. Same value, different provider paths — one authoritative mechanism per path, not two competing on one path. Interaction with the retry transport (correct, not a bug): a per-ATTEMPT read timeout raises `httpx.TimeoutException`, which is in arcticduck's retry set — so a transiently-slow model is retried; a PERSISTENTLY hung model times out each attempt, exhausts `llm_retry_max_attempts`, and surfaces `LLMUnavailableError` (classified WAIT_AND_RETRY by mamba). Bounded either way, no total-runtime cap.
- **Per-TOOL timeout — bounds ASYNC/MCP tools (an honest, verified scope).** Add `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` to the shared `Agent(...)` construction kwargs (flows to all three `Agent(model, **kwargs)` sites; a no-op on `single_turn` where `tools=[]`). VERIFIED offline: `Agent(tool_timeout=0.3)` CANCELS an ASYNC tool (`await asyncio.sleep(3)`) at ~0.3s — but is a NO-OP for a SYNC blocking tool (`time.sleep(3)` waited the full 3s), because pydantic-ai uses async cancellation which cannot interrupt sync code that blocks the loop. rebar's in-process tools (`filesystem_tools`, `rebar_tools`) are SYNC, so `tool_timeout` primarily protects MCP/network toolsets (async); the sync in-process tools are fast (file/ticket reads) and a looping/slow one is bounded instead by the step caps. When `tool_timeout` fires it CANCELS the tool and feeds a soft timeout error back to the MODEL (which adapts / can finish) — it is a liveness BOUND, not a run-aborting classified failure — it raises NO exception into the runner (so NO new classification arm is needed); the model simply receives a tool error and proceeds. This honest scope (async-tool bound + sync-tool caveat) is the correction over an earlier over-claim.
- **Step caps: ALREADY PRESENT — reused, not re-added.** The runner already derives `request_limit = max(1, ceil(max_iterations/2))` and `tool_calls_limit = max(8, max_iterations)` from `cfg.max_iterations` and applies them via `UsageLimits` on `run_sync` (pinned by arawana). Those DERIVED caps ARE the runaway-loop bound and the backstop for a repeatedly-slow sync tool; this story adds NO duplicate limit config knobs.
- **Deferred (ADR content only, NOT built):** the sub-event stream-idle-watchdog needs the async `agent.run()` migration, conflicting with the exclusive `run_sync` design. This story authors the ADR CONTENT at `docs/adr/00XX-async-liveness-watchdog.md` (placeholder number; blackbear, sole ADR owner, records/numbers it) with Context/Decision/Consequences on why it is deferred.

## Verification (probes that actually cross the mechanism)
- **Read timeout:** a MockTransport whose handler sleeps past a small `read` timeout trips `httpx.ReadTimeout` UNDER `agent.run_sync()` (the arcticduck seam), proving it fires in the synchronous runner; a MockTransport that responds JUST UNDER the read timeout completes normally (healthy-long-run not aborted).
- **Tool timeout:** an `Agent(tool_timeout=<small>)` with a deliberately-sleeping ASYNC tool is CANCELLED at ~the timeout (asserted by elapsed << tool sleep); the run continues (soft error to the model). A companion assertion documents that a SYNC blocking tool is NOT interrupted (the known caveat) — so the test pins reality, not an over-claim.
- **No total-runtime / no-asyncio:** a test greps the gate path to assert no total-runtime timer and no new event loop / `asyncio.run` in the agentic run path beyond the existing teardown.

## Scope
- ONE new `LLMConfig` key: `llm_tool_timeout_s` (default 120), read via `_llm_int` from `[llm]` + `REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S`. The per-request read timeout REUSES `cfg.timeout_s` (no new key). Regenerate `docs/env-vars.md`; add `REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S` to `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture; document both in `docs/config.md`.
- Wire `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)` into arcticduck's `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(http_timeout=...)`; add `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` to the shared `Agent(...)` kwargs.
- NOT in scope: any total-runtime cap (rejected); the async stream-event watchdog (deferred, ADR content only); new step-cap config knobs (derived caps already exist); making rebar's sync tools async or thread-cancellable (a follow-on if sync-tool bounding is ever needed).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `LLMConfig.llm_tool_timeout_s` (120) exists, read from `[llm]` + `REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S`; the per-request read timeout reuses `cfg.timeout_s` (no duplicate key). `docs/env-vars.md` regenerated (drift gate passes); `REBAR_LLM_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S` in `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture.
- [ ] `httpx.Timeout(read=cfg.timeout_s, connect=10.0, write=30.0, pool=10.0)` is passed into `_build_retrying_anthropic_model(http_timeout=...)` for both execution modes (single_turn + agentic); a sleeping MockTransport trips `httpx.ReadTimeout` under `run_sync` (probe), and a just-under-timeout MockTransport completes normally (healthy run not aborted).
- [ ] `tool_timeout=cfg.llm_tool_timeout_s` is on the shared `Agent(...)` kwargs; a sleeping ASYNC tool is cancelled at ~the timeout (probe), with a companion assertion documenting the sync-tool no-op caveat.
- [ ] No total-runtime timeout and no new asyncio event loop are introduced in the gate path (asserted by a grep/structural test); the existing derived step caps (arawana) are unchanged.
- [ ] The deferred-async-watchdog ADR CONTENT exists at `docs/adr/00XX-async-liveness-watchdog.md` (Context/Decision/Consequences; numbered/recorded by blackbear).
- [ ] `make test`/`make lint`/`make typecheck` green.

Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (build+pass `httpx.Timeout`; add `tool_timeout` to the shared Agent kwargs), `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (one LLMConfig key + `from_env`), `docs/config.md`, `docs/env-vars.md` (regenerated), `docs/adr/00XX-async-liveness-watchdog.md` (content), `tests/unit/test_config_llm.py` (`_clean`), `tests/unit/test_llm_timeouts.py` (new — read-timeout + async-tool-timeout + sync-caveat + no-total-runtime probes).

## Verified library behavior (experiment log — pydantic-ai 1.107.0, httpx 0.28.1, offline)
ACTUAL captured output (no billable call):
```
Agent.__init__ has tool_timeout: True;  Agent.run_sync has usage_limits: True
httpx.Timeout(600.0, read=300.0, connect=10.0).read == 300.0   (per-request read timeout settable)
UsageLimits fields include total_tokens_limit
Agent(tool_timeout=0.3) + ASYNC tool (await asyncio.sleep(3)) -> CANCELLED at 0.31s, run continues ('done')
Agent(tool_timeout=0.3) + SYNC  tool (time.sleep(3))        -> NOT interrupted, waited 3.02s (async cancel can't stop sync)
rebar tools (filesystem_tools/rebar_tools) are SYNC defs    -> tool_timeout bounds MCP/async tools, not these
arcticduck committed _build_retrying_anthropic_model(..., http_timeout=) in runner.py (the wiring seam)
```
These are pydantic-ai/httpx LIBRARY facts (not rebar symbols; bug succinct-formable-kite). The read
timeout on the shared client is the primary hung-MODEL bound (verified via arcticduck's spike); the
tool timeout is an honest async/MCP-tool bound with a documented sync no-op caveat.

file_impact.reason: build httpx.Timeout, pass via arcticduck http_timeout seam; add Agent tool_timeout
add the two vars to the _clean fixture

compiled_state.title: Activity-based liveness: per-request timeout + step caps + idle-watchdog (no total-runtime timeout)
